---
title: "R Episode 15 — Writing Good Software"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Apply naming, style, and structure conventions that aid reuse.
- Write automated tests with `testthat`.
- Pull the track's reproducibility practices together: style, tests, `renv`, Git, CI.

**Teaching:** 35 min · **Exercises:** 25 min
:::

This capstone episode ties the whole track together: the goal is analysis code that *another
person* (including future-you) can read, trust, and rerun.

## Style and structure

- Use descriptive `snake_case` names; functions are verbs (`load_gapminder`), data are nouns.
- Keep functions small and single-purpose (Episode 10); put them in `R/`.
- Don't repeat yourself — factor shared logic into functions.
- Format automatically and catch issues with linters:

```r
styler::style_dir("R")     # auto-format to a consistent style
lintr::lint_dir("R")       # flag style/code smells
```

## Write tests with testthat

Tests turn "I think it works" into "it's verified, and stays verified":

```r
# R/temperature.R
fahr_to_celsius <- function(temp) (temp - 32) * 5 / 9

# tests/testthat/test-temperature.R
library(testthat)
test_that("fahr_to_celsius converts known points", {
  expect_equal(fahr_to_celsius(32), 0)
  expect_equal(fahr_to_celsius(212), 100)
})
```

Run them with `testthat::test_dir("tests/testthat")` (or `devtools::test()` in a package).

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Write the test for `fahr_to_celsius` *before* fixing a deliberately broken version, so learners
see a red test go green. Tests are most convincing when they first catch a real bug.
:::

## Reproducibility, assembled

Everything from this track combines into a reproducible project:

| Practice | Tool | Episode |
|----------|------|---------|
| Self-contained project, root-relative paths | `usethis`, `here` | 2 |
| Pinned dependencies | `renv` (`renv.lock`) | 2 |
| Functions over copy-paste | functions | 10 |
| Consistent style | `styler`, `lintr` | 15 |
| Automated tests | `testthat` | 15 |
| Reproducible reports | Quarto | 14 |
| Version control + automation | Git, GitHub Actions (CI) | Foundations |

## Wire up CI

A tiny GitHub Actions workflow can run your tests on every push — the same idea as this repo's
CI. Combined with `renv::restore()`, contributors get your exact environment and immediate
feedback.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Make it fail, then pass
Write a `test_that()` for `celsius_to_kelvin()` (adds 273.15). Run it against a buggy version
that adds `273` and watch it fail; fix the function and watch it pass.

### Solution

```r
celsius_to_kelvin <- function(temp) temp + 273.15
test_that("0C is 273.15K", {
  expect_equal(celsius_to_kelvin(0), 273.15)
})
```
The buggy `+ 273` version fails `expect_equal()`; the corrected one passes.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- Readable, consistent, DRY code is reusable code — automate style with `styler`/`lintr`.
- `testthat` makes correctness verifiable and durable.
- Reproducibility is the sum of the track: projects, `here`, `renv`, functions, tests, Quarto,
  Git, and CI.
:::

This completes the **R for Reproducible Science** track. Combine it with a
[Capstone](https://github.com/FrankAsanteVanLaarhoven/ForgeLabCarpentries/blob/main/curriculum/tracks/capstones-certification.md)
to earn a micro-credential.
